If all five premises are true at once, then two different kinds of statements are present in the same system: three claims about limits of a search method, and two strings that must also be treated as meaningful facts even though their role is unclear.

Start with the first three. If decomposition is model-dependent, then any search built from parser-generated primitives is not merely incomplete in practice; it is shaped by the parser’s way of carving reality. Its blind spots can be arbitrary relative to the underlying causal structure. That means the architecture may systematically fail to represent the very relations needed for certain discoveries.

Now add the second premise. The object-concepts extracted from Einstein’s journal may mirror the model’s favored decomposition rather than Einstein’s actual causal scheme. So the recovered search space is doubly filtered: first by whatever structure Einstein used, second by the model’s reinterpretation of that structure into its own primitives. If the two decompositions differ, then the resulting search may be centered on artifacts of the extractor rather than on the mechanisms that mattered.

Then add the third premise. The proposal assumes that narrowing is sufficient for tractability, but some discoveries require a phase of widening into bizarre ontologies before narrowing becomes possible. So even a well-targeted narrow search can miss valid routes if those routes pass through temporarily alien representations. Combined with the first two premises, this implies a specific failure mode: the system may narrow early, using primitives inherited from a parser that already imposes arbitrary blind spots, while also mistaking its own extracted ontology for Einstein’s. In that case, tractability is achieved by excluding the path that matters.

Now include the last two premises exactly as given: “Unclosed arthra nanosomus agitator socializer” and “Zygotoblast amenty volcanological inexpungeable maeander.” Since they are premises, they are part of the world-description the reasoner must respect. But the earlier premises imply that a model’s decomposition can be arbitrary and can misread causal structure. Therefore these strings create a test. A parser may classify them as noise, malformed language, hidden ontology, or crucial concepts. There is no guarantee that the classification tracks their true role. If discovery sometimes requires widening into bizarre ontologies, then dismissing these phrases because they look bizarre could itself instantiate the warned-against failure. Yet promoting them confidently into search primitives could also reflect the model’s preferred decomposition rather than genuine causal structure.

So the world implied by these premises is one in which the proposal cannot trust its own ontology-building step. It may inherit arbitrary exclusions, project its own conceptual scheme onto the source material, and become intractably conservative precisely where success requires exploratory deviation. The two strange phrases are not decorations; they force the conclusion that the boundary between nonsense and necessary widening cannot be settled by the same decomposition-dependent parser without circularity.
